column2:
to write down information, especially something said aloud

e.g.
• Let me take down your phone number.
• The officer took down every detail of the witness's statement.

syn: note down, write down, record

material made of a net of wire or thread with small open spaces between

e.g.
• The window has a fine mesh to keep insects out.
• The bag is made of a light nylon mesh.

syn: net, netting

to surround or cover someone or something completely

e.g.
• Flames quickly engulfed the building.
• She was engulfed by a wave of sadness.

syn: overwhelm, swallow up, submerge

very quickly; in a very short while

e.g.
• Don't worry, we'll be there in no time.
• With the new tool, the job was done in no time.

syn: very soon, quickly, in a flash

a large group of soldiers; also an organized group of people working together

e.g.
• The fire brigade arrived within minutes.
• An army brigade was sent to the border.

syn: unit, team, squad

to help someone do something

e.g.
• A nurse assisted the doctor during surgery.
• Can you assist me with this report?

syn: help, aid, support

a small, hard, shiny white ball formed inside an oyster, used as a jewel

e.g.
• She wore a necklace of white pearls.
• Pearls form when sand gets inside an oyster's shell.

the front of a building; also a false outward appearance hiding the truth

e.g.
• The hotel has a beautiful stone facade.
• Behind his calm facade, he was very nervous.

syn: front, exterior, appearance

filled with air; or (of prices/figures) made higher than is reasonable; exaggerated

e.g.
• The prices here are really inflated.
• He gave an inflated account of his own role.

syn: exaggerated, overblown

a person or company hired to do work, especially building work

e.g.
• We hired a contractor to build the garage.
• The contractor finished the project ahead of schedule.

syn: builder

the outside of something, especially a building

e.g.
• They painted the exterior of the house white.
• The car's exterior was scratched.

syn: outside, facade

not able to be touched or measured; hard to define

e.g.
• Trust is intangible but very valuable.
• The brand's reputation is an intangible asset.

syn: abstract

to build or put up a structure

e.g.
• Workers are erecting a new bridge.
• They erected a tent in the field.

syn: build, construct, put up

money paid to a worker, usually by the hour or week

e.g.
• The average wage in this city is quite high.
• Workers demanded higher wages.

syn: pay, salary, earnings

easily set on fire

e.g.
• Keep flammable liquids away from the stove.
• The label warns that the gas is highly flammable.

syn: inflammable, combustible

to bravely deal with a difficult situation instead of avoiding it

e.g.
• He decided to stay and face it out.
• Rather than run away, she faced it out.

syn: brave it out, tough it out

to stop using or producing something gradually, in stages

e.g.
• The company will phase out the old model next year.
• They are phasing out plastic bags.

syn: discontinue, wind down

having a special quality that makes something easy to recognize

e.g.
• The bird has a distinctive red head.
• Her voice is very distinctive.

syn: unique, characteristic

to suddenly stop something; or to separate a piece by breaking

e.g.
• She broke off in the middle of her sentence.
• He broke off a piece of chocolate.

syn: stop, discontinue, snap off

well known for something bad

e.g.
• The area is infamous for crime.
• He is an infamous criminal.

syn: notorious

reasonable and showing good judgment

e.g.
• That's a sensible approach to the problem.
• She made a sensible decision.

syn: reasonable, practical, wise

willing to obey or agree; or meeting official rules and standards

e.g.
• The new car is compliant with safety rules.
• He was quiet and compliant, never arguing.

syn: obedient, conforming

already started and in progress

e.g.
• The project is now underway.
• Preparations for the event are well underway.

syn: in progress, ongoing

supervision or control; or a mistake made by not noticing something

e.g.
• Missing the deadline was an oversight on my part.
• The committee provides oversight of the budget.

syn: supervision, lapse, omission

repeatedly; many times over

e.g.
• I've told him time and time again to be careful.
• Time and time again, she proved them wrong.

syn: repeatedly, over and over, again and again

giving a lot of time and effort to something; or set aside for one special purpose

e.g.
• She's a dedicated teacher who stays late every day.
• This is a dedicated server for backups.

syn: committed, devoted

a fault or weakness; a failure to reach a good standard

e.g.
• Despite its shortcomings, the plan worked.
• He admitted his own shortcomings.

syn: weakness, flaw, defect

with regard to a particular aspect or subject

e.g.
• In terms of price, this one is better.
• The plan is risky in terms of cost.

syn: regarding, with respect to

to leave quickly; or to release a product hurriedly

e.g.
• She rushed out of the room in tears.
• They rushed out the new phone before testing it properly.

syn: dash out, hurry out

to visit each house in turn, e.g. to sell or ask for something

e.g.
• Volunteers went door to door asking for donations.
• Salesmen used to go door to door.

a group of people chosen to make decisions or manage something

e.g.
• The committee meets every Monday.
• She was elected to the planning committee.

syn: board, panel

an underlying meaning that is not stated directly

e.g.
• The subtext of her message was that she was angry.
• There's a clear subtext of jealousy in the play.

syn: undertone, implication

the act of turning; or a regular taking of turns among people or things

e.g.
• The Earth's rotation takes 24 hours.
• Nurses work in rotation, changing shifts each week.

syn: turning, cycle

to move away; or to stop pressuring or interfering

e.g.
• Back off and give him some space.
• The company backed off from the deal.

syn: retreat, withdraw, ease off

to bring back to life, consciousness, or activity

e.g.
• Doctors managed to revive the patient.
• They hope to revive the local economy.

syn: restore, resurrect, rejuvenate

to extend a hand; or to contact someone to offer or ask for help

e.g.
• Feel free to reach out if you have questions.
• She reached out to old friends online.

syn: contact, get in touch

to hold firmly in place; or to define or establish something exactly

e.g.
• It's hard to pin down the exact cause.
• The wrestler pinned his opponent down.

syn: identify, establish, specify

data shown by a device; or a summary report after a meeting or event

e.g.
• The screen gave a clear temperature readout.
• She sent a readout of the meeting to the team.

syn: display, summary, briefing

to fix in place with nails; or to settle or define something firmly

e.g.
• Let's nail down the date for the meeting.
• We need to nail down the final price.

syn: finalize, settle, confirm

a further action or contact made after something earlier

e.g.
• The doctor scheduled a follow-up next week.
• I sent a follow-up email to remind them.

syn: follow-through

to take things out of a bag or box; or to analyze a complex idea in detail

e.g.
• Let me unpack this idea a bit more.
• She unpacked her suitcase as soon as she arrived.

syn: analyze, break down

to publicly support or approve of someone or something

e.g.
• The mayor endorsed the new plan.
• A famous athlete endorses the brand.

syn: support, back, approve

quickly without delay; or exactly on time

e.g.
• She replied promptly to my email.
• The meeting started promptly at nine.

syn: quickly, immediately, punctually

helping to make something happen; favorable to

e.g.
• A quiet room is conducive to studying.
• The warm weather is conducive to plant growth.

syn: favorable, helpful

to be mentioned or arise; to approach; or (of an event) to be about to happen

e.g.
• Your name came up in the meeting.
• A problem has come up.

syn: arise, be raised

to criticize someone formally and severely for a fault

e.g.
• The manager reprimanded him for being late.
• She was reprimanded for breaking the rules.

syn: rebuke, scold, tell off

to become cooler; or to calm down after being angry or excited

e.g.
• Take a walk to cool off before you reply.
• Let the soup cool off a little.

syn: calm down, cool down

to move back to a previous position or state; to switch back

e.g.
• Let's shift back to the original plan.
• The seat shifted back when he leaned on it.

syn: move back, switch back, revert
